docs.title: FracSim
docs.text: FracSim is a fast, memory-efficient command-line tool for estimating bacterial genome similarity using the FracMinHash sketching algorithm. It computes both Jaccard index and Average Nucleotide Identity (ANI) between genomes, enabling large-scale comparative genomics studies.

docs.title: ✨ Key Features
docs.text:
• Fast: Uses FracMinHash sketching to dramatically lower memory footprint (≈33 MB per pair) and runtime.
  • Accurate: Provides Jaccard index and ANI estimates;achieves MAE < 0.25% for ANI > 95%.
  • Fully self‑contained: No dependency on BioPython, k‑mer counting libraries, or heavy bioinformatics frameworks.
  • Flexible input: Supports FASTA/FASTQ (plain or gzip/bzip2/xz/zip compressed), single or multiple files, and file lists.
  • Easy to use: Clean command‑line interface with multi‑processing support and progress indicators.
  • Multiple output formats: Table, CSV, TSV, JSON; can be saved to file.
  • Open source: MIT licensed – contributions and usage are welcome.hosted on GitHub.

docs.title: 🚀 Quick Example
docs.text:
Compare two E. coli genomes:

fracsim -i ecoli_k12.fasta ecoli_o157.fasta -k 21 -s 100 --ani

docs.title: FracMinHash Sketching
docs.text:
FracMinHash is a variant of the MinHash technique designed for comparing datasets of different sizes. The process consists of the following steps:

  1. k‑mer extraction: The genome sequence is split into all overlapping substrings of length k (k‑mers).
  2. Hashing: Each k‑mer is hashed to a 64‑bit integer using MurmurHash3 (with a user‑supplied seed).
  3. Sampling: Only those hashes that are smaller than a threshold max_hash = floor(2⁶⁴ // scaled) are retained. This ensures that approximately a fraction scaled of all k‑mers are kept, independent of genome length.
  4. Sketch: The set of retained hashes is the genome sketch.

docs.title: Jaccard Index
docs.text:
Given two sketches A and B, the Jaccard index is defined as:

[ J(A,B) = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|} ]

It ranges from 0 (no shared k‑mers) to 1 (identical sets of k‑mers). FracSim uses the sketches to estimate this value directly.

docs.title: ANI Estimation
docs.text:
Average Nucleotide Identity (ANI) is approximated from the Jaccard index using the relationship derived in the Mash paper (Ondov et al., 2016):

[ \text{ANI} \approx 1 - \frac{1}{k} \ln\left(\frac{2J}{1+J}\right) ]

where k is the k‑mer length and J is the Jaccard index. This formula assumes that mutations are randomly distributed and that k‑mers are unique. For very low similarities (J < 0.1), the estimate becomes less reliable.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Ondov, B. D., et al. (2016). Mash: fast genome and metagenome distance estimation using MinHash. Genome Biology, 17(1), 132.
  • Irber, L. C., et al. (2020). Decentralizing indices for genomic data. University of California, Davis.

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text:
All notable changes to FracSim are documented in this file.

The format is based on Keep a Changelog, and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning.

docs.title: Added
docs.text: • (Planned features for next release)

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: • (Changes in existing functionality)

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • (Bug fixes)

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Bioconda release: FracSim is now available via conda install -c bioconda fracsim.
  • GitHub Actions CI/CD:
  • Automated unit testing (pytest) on Python 3.8–3.14 across Linux, macOS, and Windows.
  • Support TSV: Add TSV output format
  • Performance monitoring: -p, --performance flag to report total time and peak memory usage.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text: • Default kmer-size remains 16 (optimal based on benchmarking).

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text: • Critical: Fixed signed/unsigned mismatch in MurmurHash3 that caused incorrect sampling (FracMinHash threshold not applied properly).

docs.title: Contributing to FracSim
docs.text: We welcome contributions of all forms: bug reports, feature requests, documentation improvements, and code contributions.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository on GitHub.
  2. Clone your fork locally: ```bash git clone https://github.com/your-username/FracSim.git cd FracSim

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text:
• Python: 3.8 or higher
  • Operating System: Linux, macOS, Windows (WSL recommended)

docs.title: Method 1: Install via Conda (recommended)
docs.text:
The easiest way to install FracSim is via conda from the bioconda channel:

conda install -c bioconda fracsim

docs.title: Method 2: Install From PyPI
docs.text: pip install FracSim

docs.title: Method 3: Download binaries (no Python required)
docs.text: Check out the Releases Page for pre-built binaries across all supported platforms.

docs.title: Method 4: Install from source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/zhuyu534/FracSim.git
cd FracSim
pip install -e .

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks you through the simplest use case: comparing two bacterial genomes.

docs.title: Prepare Input Files
docs.text:
Create two FASTA files (or use your own). For example:

cat > ecoli_k12.fasta << 'EOF'
>Escherichia_coli_K12_MG1655
AGCTTTTCATTCTGACTGCAACGGGCAATATGTCTCTGTGTGGATTAAAAAAAGAG
TGTCTGATAGCAGCTTCTGAACTGGTTACCTGCCGTGAGTAAATTAAAATTTTATT
GACTTAGGTCACTAAATACTTTAACCAATATAGGCATAGCGCACAGACAGATAAAA
EOF

cat > ecoli_o157.fasta << 'EOF'
>Escherichia_coli_O157_H7
GGCTTTTCATTCTGACTGCAACGGGCAATATGTCTCTGTGTGGATTAAAAAAAGAG
TGTCTGATAGCAGCTTCTGAACTGGTTACCTGCCGTGAGTAAATTAAAATTTTATT
GACTTAGGTCACTAAATACTTTAACCAATATAGGCATAGCGCACAGACAGATCCCC
EOF

docs.title: Run Example
docs.text: fracsim -i ecoli_k12.fasta ecoli_o157.fasta --ani -k 15 -s 1000 -V

docs.title: Command Line Options
docs.text: Argument Description -i, --input Space-separated list of input genome files (FASTA/Q). -l, --list File containing one input file path per line. -k, --kmer-size k-mer length (1–64, default: 16). -s, --scaled FracMinHash sampling rate (integer >= 1, default: 100). --seed Random seed for hashing (default: 42). -a, --ani Compute ANI in addition to Jaccard index (Percentage). -t, --threads Number of parallel tasks (default: 1). -f, --format Output format: table, csv, json, tsv (default: table). -o, --output Output file path (default: stdout). -m, --min-similarity Minimum similarity threshold (Jaccard index, or ANI if --ani is set),Only output pairs with similarity ≥ this value (default: 0.0). -p, --performance Enable performance monitoring (total time and peak memory) - high self overhead, may slow down computation. -V, --verbose Print detailed progress information. -v, --version Show program version and exit. -h, --help Show this help message and exit.

docs.title: Input Options
docs.text: Option Description -i, --input Space-separated list of input genome files. -l, --list File containing one input file path per line. Alternative to -i for handling many files.

docs.title: Supported file formats
docs.text:
• FASTA (.fasta, .fa, .fna, .ffn, .frn): sequences only.
  • FASTQ (.fastq, .fq): sequences and quality scores (quality is ignored for sketching).
  • Compressed files (.gz, .bz2, .xz, .zip): all above formats can be compressed and will be auto-detected and decompressed on the fly.

docs.title: k-mer Settings
docs.text: Option Description -k, --kmer-size k-mer length (1–64, default: 31). Longer k-mers provide higher specificity but require more memory. -s, --scaled FracMinHash sampling rate (integer >= 1, default: 100). Controls sketch size: lower values = smaller sketches (faster but less sensitive). --seed Random seed for hashing (default: 42). Change to get different hash values for the same input.

docs.title: Output Control
docs.text: Option Description -a, --ani Compute ANI (Average Nucleotide Identity) in addition to Jaccard index. Adds ANI column to output. -f, --format Output format: table, csv, json, tsv (default: table). Console always displays formatted results. -o, --output Output file path. Format is auto-detected from extension (.csv, .json, .txt, .tsv). -m, --min-similarity Filter results: only output pairs with similarity ≥ this value (default: 0.0). Useful for focusing on high-similarity genomes.

docs.title: Output Formats
docs.text:
FracSim supports multiple output formats to suit different needs:

  • Table: Human-readable formatted table
  • CSV: Comma-separated values, ideal for spreadsheet software or further analysis
  • JSON: Structured data format, perfect for programmatic processing and integration
  • TSV: Tab separated, machine friendly, can be directly imported by tools such as Excel, R, Python (pandas), etc.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text: Option Description -t, --threads Number of parallel tasks (sketch generation phase is accelerated using multiprocessing), default 1.

docs.title: Information
docs.text: Option Description -V, --verbose Print detailed progress information, including sketch sizes and processing time. -v, --version Show program version and exit. -h, --help Show this help message and exit.

docs.title: Basic pairwise comparison
docs.text: fracsim -i genome1.fna genome2.fna -k 31 -s 100 --ani

docs.title: Batch processing multiple files
docs.text:
# Using space-separated list
fracsim -i genome1.fna genome2.fna genome3.fna -k 31 -s 1000
# Using list file
fracsim -l genome_list.txt -k 31 -s 1000

docs.title: Save results as CSV
docs.text: fracsim -i genome1.fna genome2.fna -k 31 -s 100 --ani -o results.csv

docs.title: Filter high-similarity pairs only
docs.text: fracsim -i genome1.fna genome2.fna -k 31 -s 100 --min-similarity 80

docs.title: JSON output with verbose logging
docs.text: fracsim -i genome1.fna genome2.fna -k 31 -s 100 --format json -o results.json -V

docs.title: Parallel processing of large genomes
docs.text: fracsim -i large_genome.fna reference_genome.fna -k 31 -s 100 --threads 8

docs.title: Working with compressed files
docs.text:
# Auto-detects and decompresses .gz files
fracsim -i genome1.fna.gz genome2.fna.gz -k 31 -s 100 --ani
